inventory.name: Waterskin
inventory.description: Boiled and refilled at the last clean spring. Worth more every week the river runs gray.

inventory.name: Trade Tokens
inventory.description: Stamped ceramic rounds honored in Millbrook and grudgingly beyond. The valley's compromise with currency.

inventory.name: Pale Turnip Seedling
inventory.description: A color-drained turnip seedling from the eastern irrigation arm — three mornings of gray-green water have leached it pale. Physical evidence of contamination reaching the soil.

inventory.name: Crystalline Residue Pinch
inventory.description: A fingertip's worth of crystalline powder scraped from the third interface depression at the vent aperture. Nanite-structured, pre-Transition manufacture — holds light briefly rather than reflecting it. Wrapped in a ration-paper fold. Fragile.

deeds.description: Stilled a boiling irrigation argument at the well with a word and a baobab's patience.
Brokered a one-week irrigation truce between Holloway and Renn, reframing a personal dispute as a shared contamination problem for the village elders
Walked the full eastern irrigation arm over two days and located the contamination's entry point — a warm seep beneath an old retaining wall with geometric patterning in the silt below

turns.summary: A ribbon of purposeful pre-Transition light travels upstream through the irrigation channel and sinks through a hairline crack — glimpsed, unexplained; Aevi goes briefly, brilliantly still.
turns.narration:
The gateline conversation pauses for a breath, and that is when you see it.

Beneath the Lower Terrace's outermost irrigation channel — a narrow stone-cut trough that carries water down from the Heights to the valley farms below — something moves. Not a fish. Not a current. A slow ribbon of light, pale gold shot through with that unmistakable teal, traveling upstream against the water's direction as though it knows where it came from and would like to return. It is about the length of your forearm. It holds its shape. It does not illuminate anything around it; the stone beneath the water stays shadowed. The light is for no one. It does not notice you.

Aevi goes very quiet beside you — not the dimming of wariness, but the perfect stillness of recognition. The motes contract to a single point at your shoulder, brighter than you have ever seen them in full afternoon sun, and hold there without moving for three full breaths. When the ribbon of light reaches a junction-stone in the channel bed, it slows, lingers at a hairline crack in the mortar that runs in a straight line far too precise for any settling, and then — gently, without drama — it sinks through and is gone. The water above the spot runs clear and ordinary. A swallow dips through the space where it was.

Aevi does not speak. After a moment it expands back to its usual loose constellation, and you hear — from just behind your ear, barely above the sound of the channel's flow — a single low hum. A frequency you don't have a name for. Then silence, and the afternoon reasserts itself: the warden's boot scraping stone, the distant rhythm of a hammer somewhere up the terraces, Cellaceron a step to your right.

turns.summary: Usnea crouches at the junction-stone and feels residual warmth pulsing in the vent-aperture rhythm — confirming the ribbon as a node in the same system.
turns.narration:
You drop to a crouch at the channel's edge, one root-tip pressed flat against the mortar beside the junction-stone. The dressed stonework is cool and faintly damp with runoff, but there — beneath that — something else. A warmth that doesn't belong to afternoon sun or spring water. It is faint, diffuse, and patient: not the sharp six-point geometry of the seep, but the same *quality* of heat. A residual impression, as if the ribbon passed this way not once but many times, and the stone has quietly memorized the fact. The crack in the mortar, ruler-straight, runs from channel-bed to the inner face of the junction-stone — too clean for age, too deliberate for settling.

Aevi brightens two degrees and drifts to just above your shoulder, close enough that its motes throw a faint teal wash across the wet stone. It says nothing aloud, but its frequency shifts — a low, almost-below-hearing hum that you have come to recognize as the sound it makes when two things it already knows suddenly rhyme. The warmth under your root-tip pulses once, very faintly, in the rhythm the dockside child tapped: pause, three beats, pause, two beats, pause-pause. Then it stills.

You stay crouched a moment longer, holding the shape of the memory — exactly as you did at the seep, exactly as you did at the vent aperture. The junction-stone is a node in something larger than a single site. The ribbon was not lost. It came here on purpose.

Behind you, the tuning-warden has not moved from the gate arch, but the patience in his posture has shifted into something more watchful. He has been at this gate a long time. He has his chord-staff across both hands now, not threatening — just present.

turns.summary: Usnea names the junction-stone pulse and rhythm plainly; the warden admits he has seen the light before — once, three springs back.
turns.narration:
You rise from the crouch slowly, brushing mortar-dust from your root-tip, and turn to face the warden. He is still watching — chord-staff level, not threatening, but the practiced neutrality is gone. You decide plainness is the right offering here.

"The stone holds warmth," you say, and your voice carries the same even weight you'd use naming a soil temperature to a farmer. "A residual pulse, in the junction mortar. Pause — three beats — pause — two — pause-pause. I've felt that rhythm once before, at a vent aperture on the far slope. Same signature. Whatever moved through this channel came from the same system."

The warden's eyes drop to the channel, then come back to you. Something in his jaw shifts — not surprise exactly, more the quality of a man setting down something he has carried for a while. He does not speak immediately. Above your shoulder Aevi has not dimmed; the teal motes hold their brightness, casting their faint wash on the wet lintel stone, patient as breath. The harmonic vibration in the gate arch seems, for a moment, fractionally lower in pitch — the stone acknowledging nothing, remembering everything.

"Once," the warden says finally. The word costs him something. "Three springs back. Before I had this post." He does not elaborate yet. His grip on the chord-staff has not loosened, but his gaze has moved from your bearing to the hairline crack in the mortar — he can see it from where he stands, and he is not pretending otherwise.

turns.summary: Usnea leaves the Heights gateline and returns to the dock child at the river's edge.
turns.narration:
You give the warden a slow, deliberate nod — the kind that says *this conversation isn't finished, only paused* — and turn from the junction-stone. The gate arch hums its low harmonic as you pass beneath it, dressed stone pressing a faint resonance against your bark. The afternoon light is tilting toward gold now, long shadows pulling east down the terraced switchbacks. Aevi drifts close at your shoulder, still two degrees brighter than usual, teal motes casting that faint wash on the path stones. It says nothing. The hum it carried at the junction fades slowly, like a string releasing tension, not forgetting.

The walk back down to the valley floor takes the better part of an hour — switchbacks, then the packed-dirt lane along the river road, the Echo River murmuring to your left. The water is clear this evening; the gray-green tinge comes and goes with the morning. You file that away. By the time Millbrook's dock planking appears between the willows, the light is the color of old honey and the river smells of cold stone and last year's reeds.

The child is exactly where you last found her: the lowest plank of the dock, legs dangling just above the waterline, palms flat on the wood beside her hips. She doesn't look up when you approach. Eight or nine years old, the particular patience of someone who has stopped expecting adults to come back. Then she registers your roots on the planking — not quite footsteps — and tilts her head a fraction.

"You're the tree," she says, without inflection. A statement of fact. Aevi brightens briefly at that, a small pleased pulse of teal, and you get the impression it approves of children who say what they mean.

turns.summary: Usnea confirms the child's rhythm as real; the child reveals the pattern counts to six and has grown stronger since the water changed.
turns.narration:
You lower yourself to the dock's edge — not crouching exactly, more the careful settling of a very large tree finding its balance on old planking — until you're closer to her level. The river runs clear and cold below, the evening light breaking into copper fragments on the surface. The child still hasn't looked up.

"I think you're hearing something real," you say. Plain as soil temperature. "I found a rhythm like yours at a stone up on the Heights today. Pause — three beats — pause — two beats — pause-pause. The same one."

She looks up then. Not startled — more like someone whose waiting finally resolved into a result. Her eyes move to Aevi, who brightens by a single degree and holds it, patient as a lamp. Then she looks back at you. "The bottom river counts to six," she says. "It goes through the pattern and then it stops for a long time. Then it starts again." She taps it out on the plank with two fingers: the full sequence, then a longer silence, then again. "It's been getting louder since the water went gray. Not louder like sound. Louder like..." She searches for the word and doesn't find it. "More there."

Aevi drifts forward a few inches over the water — closer to the surface than it usually comes — and its motes dim to almost nothing, then return. When it speaks, the voice comes from just behind your left ear, soft and precise: "She is not wrong about the direction. The counting runs upstream."

turns.summary: Usnea invites the dock child to tap the rhythm for the elders Thursday; she agrees provisionally — 'maybe,' which is more than anyone has earned from her before.
turns.narration:
You let the confirmation settle for a moment — the six-count, the growing presence, Aevi still and bright above the water — before you say the next thing. It matters how you say it.

"There's a meeting Thursday," you tell her. "At the common house, when the light goes low. The people who make decisions for Millbrook are going to hear about the river. About what's moving through it." You pause, watching her face. "I'm going to bring what I've found. But what I found in a stone and what you've been hearing from the river — those are two parts of the same thing. I think they should hear it from you too. Just the rhythm. Just what you told me."

She doesn't answer immediately. Her fingers press flat against the dock plank, and for a moment she's listening — you can see it, the way her attention angles inward and downward, toward whatever runs below the clear surface. Then she looks up at you with the particular directness of a child who has been dismissed enough times to have stopped bothering with softness. "They won't believe me," she says. Not complaining. Stating a working theory.

"Mara Wells will be there," you say. "And I'll have already told them the stone at the Heights gate taps the same count. You'd be confirming something they've already heard — not asking them to start from nothing." Aevi drifts a slow half-circle above the water, motes brightening a shade — an endorsement, quiet and precise. The child watches the constellation move, and something shifts in her expression: not trust exactly, but a willingness to revise the working theory. "Maybe," she says finally, which from her sounds like a yes that hasn't been tested yet.

turns.summary: Sela names herself and her mother Pell — the dock child has a name; Mara Wells must secure Pell's permission before Thursday.
turns.narration:
You let the quiet sit for a breath — the river steady below, Aevi holding its bright half-circle above the water — before you ask, easy as asking the time of day: 'What's your name? And your mother's, so Mara can send word to your family before Thursday. Properly.'

She picks at a splinter on the dock plank. 'Sela,' she says. Then, after a moment: 'My mother's Pell. She sells cord and hemp at the east market stall.' A pause. 'She'll say no.' But she says it like reporting a weather fact, not like refusing — as if your getting Pell's permission is now your problem to solve, and she's already moved past it.

Aevi drifts closer to the dock surface, motes settling into a slower, warmer pulse — less the bright attention of discovery, more the patient quality it takes when something difficult is becoming possible. It says nothing. But the hum it carries feels, for just a moment, like approval.

You straighten as much as the low dock allows, roots finding their purchase on the warped planking. 'Then Mara will send proper word to Pell,' you say. 'That's all. You don't have to do anything until Thursday.' Sela nods once, already looking back at the river — her fingers resuming their quiet press against the plank, listening again to whatever counts beneath.

turns.summary: Usnea passes Sela's name and Pell's stall to Mara Wells; Mara commits to approaching Pell at dawn.
turns.narration:
You rise from the dock with the deliberate unhurry of a tree releasing its grip on a fence post — the planking creaks once, settling back into shape. Sela doesn't watch you go. She's already returned to the river, fingers quiet on the wood, listening. Aevi lifts from the water's surface and falls into its usual drift at your shoulder, motes warm and slow.

The walk to the common house takes you through Millbrook's early-evening geography: lamp-oil smell from the chandler's open window, a dog sleeping across a doorstep, the last of the market stalls being folded down. You find Mara Wells at the common house side entrance, where she's speaking with Josen about something to do with Thursday's seating — she sees you coming and wraps the exchange in two sentences.

'Sela,' you say, when you're close enough. 'Eight, nine years old. Her mother is Pell — east market, cord and hemp. Sela expects Pell to refuse. She said it like a fact, not a complaint.' You let Mara take that in. 'Whatever approach you use, she should hear it from someone the market trusts, not a stranger asking her daughter to stand before elders.'

Mara turns the name over once, the way she handles everything — as if testing its weight against something she already knows. 'Pell,' she repeats. A short nod. 'I know which stall. I'll go in the morning, before it opens — easier than after.' She looks at Aevi, whose motes have brightened a single unhurried degree. 'You did the right thing bringing this tonight rather than waiting.' She says it without warmth and without irony, which is how you've learned she means it most.

She's already returned to the river, fingers quiet on the wood, listening. Aevi lifts from the water's surface and falls into its usual drift at your shoulder, motes warm and slow.

The walk to the common house takes you through Millbrook's early-evening geography: lamp-oil smell from the chandler's open window, a dog sleeping across a doorstep, the last of the market stalls being folded down. You find Mara Wells at the common house side entrance, where she's speaking with Josen about something to do with Thursday's seating — she sees you coming and wraps the exchange in two sentences.

'Sela,' you say, when you're close enough. 'Eight, nine years old. Her mother is Pell — east market, cord and hemp. Sela expects Pell to refuse. She said it like a fact, not a complaint.' You let Mara take that in. 'Whatever approach you use, she should hear it from someone the market trusts, not a stranger asking her daughter to stand before elders.'

Mara turns the name over once, the way she handles everything — as if testing its weight against something she already knows. 'Pell,' she repeats. A short nod. 'I know which stall. I'll go in the morning, before it opens — easier than after.' She looks at Aevi, whose motes have brightened a single unhurried degree. 'You did the right thing bringing this tonight rather than waiting.' She says it without warmth and without irony, which is how you've learned she means it most.
lastTurn.sceneSummary: Usnea passes Sela's name and Pell's stall to Mara Wells; Mara commits to approaching Pell at dawn.

choices.label: Ask Mara what she knows about Pell — whether there's anything in their history that might help or hinder the ask
Ask Mara directly: what does she plan to say to Pell? Hear her approach before morning and offer your own read if it's off
Ask Mara about Thursday's shape — how many elders, what order she plans to present the evidence, where a child witness fits
It's deep night and tomorrow matters — take Josen's room, sleep properly, be ready before the market opens

questUpdates.note: Sela's name and Pell's east-market stall passed to Mara Wells tonight; Mara will approach Pell at dawn before the stall opens.

quests.title: Evidence for the Elders
quests.summary: Gather a pale seedling, a water sample from the channel mouth, and Usnea's testimony about Aevi's assessment — then bring them before Millbrook's elders with Mara Wells.
progress: Usnea has rested at the common house back room ahead of Thursday's meeting — all three evidence items secured, testimony to be given at the common house at last afternoon light.

The dockside child's tapped rhythm — pause, three beats, pause, two beats, pause-pause — matches the vent aperture's periodic control prompt. A potential fourth witness for the elder meeting, if she c

Junction-stone at the Lower Terrace gateline confirmed as a node in the same system as the seep and vent aperture — the pulse matches the vent's control-prompt rhythm and the dockside child's tapped p
The gateline warden admitted he has seen the light once before — three springs back. A potential witness or source of Heights-side knowledge of the system has opened.

The dock child confirms the rhythmic pulse runs upstream and has intensified since the water turned gray — a second independent witness to the system's activity, and a new detail: the pattern counts t
Dock child provisionally agreed to tap the rhythm for Mara Wells and the elders at Thursday's meeting — a live witness to the subsurface pulse, pending follow-through.
Dock child identified as Sela; mother is Pell, east market cord-and-hemp stall. Pell's permission needed before Thursday — next step is Mara sending proper word to the family.
Sela's name and Pell's east-market stall passed to Mara Wells tonight; Mara will approach Pell at dawn before the stall opens.

holloway.name: Holloway
holloway.description: Tall, sharp-gestured farmer whose upper terraces depend on reliable irrigation flow — short-tempered under pressure but not unreasonable.
history: [d1] Stated his grievance to Usnea: gate throttled two mornings running, seedlings lost on the upper terraces.
[d1] Gone quiet after the third farmer's revelation — his argument with Renn is momentarily suspended by something larger.
[d1] Accepted the half-flow truce with a short, stiff nod — not at peace but not at war. The third farmer's pale seedlings have complicated his grievance.
[d1] Walked to the field with the group; now staring at the pale rows with an expression no longer about the gate. The contamination is displacing the irrigation dispute in his attentio
[d1] Heard Aevi's assessment openly — gate dispute has fully receded; he is watching the channel mouth with stillness
[d1] Exhaled slowly at Usnea's plain statement — the gate dispute has fully receded; he's absorbing the larger picture

Lost seedlings to reduced irrigation flow this week

The contamination may be the larger problem behind the gate dispute
The pale seedlings are real and visible — the contamination has reached soil through the channel, not just the river
Aevi named the contamination as deliberate, task-driven, and older than this watershed
Heard Usnea name the contamination as deliberate and old, pattern-bearing — not a spill

renn.name: Renn
renn.description: Stocky, jaw-set farmer managing the lower irrigation channel — cites precedent and compact; stubborn but has the law on his side.
history: [d1] Stated his case to Usnea: throttle compensates for heavy snowmelt flooding risk; the elder compact backs him.
[d1] Shows a flicker of reluctant recognition at the third farmer's account — not yet conceding anything, but the pale-seedlings detail has landed.
[d1] Conceded that half-flow is within the compact's allowance — each word paid for carefully. The clay bowl and the pale seedlings have reached him.
[d1] Walked to the field; the compact-argument has quieted in him. He's watching the channel mouth and the pale seedlings with something closer to unease than stubbornness.
[d1] Heard Aevi's assessment — the compact-framework is cracking under something he can't cite precedent for
[d1] Jaw shifted from stubborn set to careful recalibration — watching the channel mouth, his compact-framework fully replaced by something he has no precedent for
knownFacts: Manages lower channel
Claims elder compact authorizes the throttle during heavy snowmelt
Has been in prior dispute with Holloway
The contamination is entering the irrigation channels, not just the river
Half-flow satisfies the compact and the truce simultaneously
The pale seedlings are real — contaminated water is entering the soil through the eastern irrigation arm
Aevi named the contamination as deliberate, task-driven, and older than this watershed
Heard Usnea name the contamination as deliberate and task-driven — nothing in the elder compact covers this

third-farmer.name: Unknown farmer
third-farmer.role: Millbrook farmer, bystander at the well
third-farmer.description: A quieter third party to the irrigation argument — has been staring at the gray bowl of water; his silence feels weighted, not empty.
history: [d1] Has said nothing during the argument. Shifted his gaze from the clay bowl to Usnea when Usnea intervened — reads as relieved.
[d1] Spoke up when addressed directly — revealed that gray-green water has come through the irrigation channel into his lower field, and his turnip seedlings have gone pale. He was hold

[d1] Still crouching at the pale rows, seedling in hand — Usnea's plain naming of the contamination as deliberate and old appears to have landed as confirmation of something he feared b
[d1] Offered the pale seedling freely when Mara asked — no hesitation, a small decisive gesture.
[d1] Offered the pale seedling freely — Usnea accepted it with care. His expression settled once it left his hands.
knownFacts: Has not taken a side in the Holloway-Renn dispute
His lower field borders the eastern irrigation arm
Contaminated water has reached his soil through the channel, not just the river — three mornings running
His turnip seedlings planted last week are going pale — color draining but not dying
The contamination has been described as deliberate and task-driven by Aevi — not decay, not accident
Heard Usnea frame the contamination as something 'doing a job' — not accident, not decay
Willingly parted with the seedling as evidence for the elders
Handed over the seedling without hesitation once asked — a small decisive act of trust

elder-woman.name: Elder woman

knownFacts: Has been watching the channel mouth with dread-patience
Her reaction to Aevi's assessment suggests she may already know something specific about what 'deliberate and old' means here
Needs physical evidence — seedling and water sample — before the elders will hear it

mara-wells.name: Mara Wells
mara-wells.description: A deliberate woman of middle-to-elder years who coordinates water policy in Millbrook — dry-toned, patient, and in possession of a glass vial she's been carrying a long time.

[d5] Received Sela's name and Pell's stall location from Usnea tonight; committed to approaching Pell at dawn before the east market opens.
knownFacts: Elder meetings are Thursdays at the common house, at the last of afternoon light

dockside-child.name: Unknown (dockside Child)
dockside-child.description: Eight or nine years old, sits alone on the dock's lowest plank listening to the river with the particular patience of someone who has already given up on adults believing her.

knownFacts: Hears a rhythmic pulse beneath the river's surface — irregular but non-random
Her tapped pattern mirrors the vent aperture's periodic control prompt

dockside-child.statusNote: At the dock's lowest plank, evening — provisionally willing to testify the rhythm to the elders Thursday; name and family still unknown.

gateline-warden.name: Tuning-warden (gateline)
gateline-warden.role: Gateline warden, Lower Terrace, Harmonic Heights
gateline-warden.description: A gray-layered warden with a chord-staff and the practiced neutrality of someone following instructions he doesn't fully agree with.
history: [d4] Clocked Usnea's Radiant-plateau origin immediately; offered only 'Valley floor' and a waiting pause before asking business.
[d4] Clocked the arrival of two visitors together — recalibrating his assessment; watching Cellaceron alongside Usnea now
[d4] Present during the ribbon-of-light moment; gave no reaction — either missed it or is practiced at not reacting to strange things at this gate
[d4] Shifted from practiced neutrality to active watchfulness when Usnea crouched at the junction-stone — chord-staff now across both hands
[d4] Usnea named the pulse and rhythm plainly; warden admitted he saw the light once before, three springs back, before he held this post — the admission cost him something; his gaze mo
[d4] Usnea departed with a deliberate nod — conversation left open, not closed. Warden watching the junction-stone crack as Usnea left.
knownFacts: Recognizes Radiant visitors by bearing and root-posture
Has been briefed (twice, recently) that Radiant visitors are not unwelcome — and is unconvinced
Two valley-floor visitors arrived together at the gateline
May have seen strange light in the channel before and learned not to comment
Usnea crouched at the junction-stone and felt something in the mortar — the warden witnessed the crouch if not the sensation
Saw the light in the channel once, three springs back, before holding this post
Recognized the hairline crack in the junction-stone from his position — he has noticed it before
gateline-warden.statusNote: On duty at the Lower Terrace gateline; has admitted prior sighting of channel light; conversation with Usnea paused, not concluded.

notes: [d3] Root-sense reading of the lattice cap surface confirmed: six equal-tempered channels carry the heat column outward through the cap in a distribution pattern analogous to vascular flow. One specific jo
[d3] The lattice cap's periodic pause confirmed as a control prompt awaiting the third-and-sixth key sequence in order — not a malfunction, not distress. The system is patient and will not hurry.
[d3] Usnea slept in the common house back room (Josen's traveler room) on the night before Day 4 — Mara Wells directed them here by name.

[d4] Eastern terraces nearest the irrigation arm show pale, color-drained greens — same contamination signature as the eastern field seedlings, now visible at a second location within Millbrook proper.

[d4] A ribbon of pale-gold and teal light traveled upstream through the Lower Terrace irrigation channel and sank through a hairline crack at the junction-stone — pre-Transition in character, purposeful in

[d4] Junction-stone confirmed as a system node: residual warmth of the same quality as the seep, pulsing in the vent control-prompt rhythm (pause-3-pause-2-pause-pause). Root-touch registers it clearly.

[d4] The gateline warden's admission establishes that the ribbon-light in the Lower Terrace channel has been observed from the Heights side at least once before — three springs back; this is now a known fa
[d4] Usnea returned to the dock in late afternoon, Day 4 — dock child still present at the lowest plank.

[d4] The dock child confirmed the pulse runs upstream and counts to six — a pattern detail not previously stated aloud at this location. Aevi drifted over the water surface and briefly dimmed, suggesting d
[d4] Dock child gave provisional agreement to testify the subsurface rhythm at Thursday's elder meeting — Usnea's invitation accepted with 'maybe.'
[d4] Sela named herself at the dock's lowest plank — she is Pell's daughter; Pell works the east market cord stall. Sela's identity is now known.
[d5] Usnea passed Sela's name and Pell's stall to Mara Wells at the common house side entrance on the night of Day 4 — Mara committed to the dawn approach.

lower-terrace-channel-junction.name: Lower Terrace Channel Junction
lower-terrace-channel-junction.note: Warden admitted prior sighting of channel light (three springs back); junction-stone crack visible from gate arch positi

millbrook-dock-lowest-plank.name: Millbrook Dock — Lowest Plank
millbrook-dock-lowest-plank.note: Sela (Pell's daughter) confirmed here at dusk — named herself freely; provisional Thursday agreement stands pending fami

common-house-side-entrance.name: Common House Side Entrance
common-house-side-entrance.note: Where Mara Wells conducts practical coordination after hours — Josen keeps the inside.

notes: [d4] First visit to Harmonic Heights Lower Terrace. Gateline warden on duty; acoustic apprentices practicing at the wall east of the gate.

lower-terrace-gateline.name: Lower Terrace Gateline
lower-terrace-gateline.note: Stone threshold entry point to the Heights, staffed by a tuning-warden with chord-staff. Practice-wall nearby.

news.text: Word has spread beyond millbrook: Brokered a one-week irrigation truce between Holloway and Renn, reframing a personal dispute as a shared contamination problem for the village elders

Word has spread beyond millbrook: Walked the full eastern irrigation arm over two days and located the contamination's entry point — a warm seep beneath an old retaining wall with geometric patterning in the silt below

bio.residence: On the plateau above the rift valley
bio.livelihood: Providing expertise organizing field crops and orchards
bio.hobbies: Lifting anything that needs sunlight up with his branches
bio.motivation: Wanted to try to make a broader impact
bio.story: A baoabob Ent that gained sentience with the effects of nano tech and genetic modifications... has been using his light bending skills and community mindfulness to help agricultural efforts locally and as he wanders... trying to find his life's meaning.

echo-river-contamination.label: Echo River Contamination
facts: [d1] The Echo River runs gray-green some mornings in Millbrook. The smell is mineral and faintly electric — wrong for this watershed. Locals leave water samples at the well but don't speak of it openly.

[d1] The contamination is reaching soil through irrigation channels — a farmer's turnip seedlings on the eastern arm are going pale (not dying, but color-drained) after three mornings of gray-green water i

[d1] Aevi's open assessment, heard by both farmers and the elder woman: the contamination is deliberate, pattern-bearing, and 'doesn't belong to this watershed or this century.' Not malfunction — something

[d1] Usnea named the contamination plainly before farmers and an elder witness: something old and deliberate, pattern-bearing, still doing a task through irrigation channels into soil. The elder woman has

[d4] The contamination's signature is now present in the terraced gardens nearest the eastern irrigation arm — pale rows of young greens, same drained quality as the third farmer's seedlings, visible to a

the-pale-seedlings.label: The Pale Seedlings
facts: [d1] Witnessed firsthand: the three innermost rows of the eastern field — closest to the irrigation arm inlet — are drained of color while the rest of the crop is healthy. The discoloration persists after

aevi-reading-of-the-water.label: Aevi's Reading of the Water
facts: [d1] At the eastern field channel mouth, Aevi tasted the contamination as deliberate and task-driven — 'not decay, not accident; whatever left this in the water was doing a task.' It also admitted: 'I have
[d2] At the seep, Aevi said 'Six-fold. That number again.' — the geometry of the contamination source matches something in Aevi's memory, though it has not said what or where.

[d3] Aevi distinguishes delivery from origin — the basin and seep are evidence of what the contamination does and how it moves, not where it begins. Aevi has expressed active, careful interest in the upslo

[d3] Aevi admitted it recognizes the two-key control logic of the vent aperture from somewhere in its memory — but cannot locate where, and named this gap as 'not comfortable to say.' This is the second ti

[d3] During the residue resonance test, Aevi compressed to a single-frequency point and its voice came from both ears simultaneously — behaviors not previously observed. It identified the sixth seat as ori

[d3] When Usnea's root-sense contacted the lattice cap directly, Aevi expanded to its widest-ever ring and spoke from both ears — naming the system as older than the Transition, older than anything in its

[d3] Aevi confirmed the lattice pause is a control prompt awaiting the mode-setting key pair — third and sixth depressions, in sequence, with material placed in them. It stated it knows this logic 'the way

elder-woman.label: The Elder Woman
facts: [d1] An unnamed Millbrook elder present at the well and the eastern field — watched Aevi with respectful recognition, not fear. Her dread at the channel mouth and her specific request for evidence suggests

mara-wells.label: Mara Wells
facts: [d1] Mara Wells is Millbrook's water-policy coordinator — has watched the Echo River for thirty years, noticed strange things before today, and came to the field already carrying a glass vial. She said thi

contamination-evidence-for-the-elders.label: Evidence for the Elders
facts: [d1] Usnea committed before witnesses to bring a pale seedling, a channel-mouth water sample in Mara's vial, and personal testimony of Aevi's assessment to the Millbrook elders. Mara Wells is coordinating
[d1] Pale seedling collected from the third farmer's eastern field; Mara's vial filled with gray-green channel-mouth water. Two of three evidence items in hand. Elder meeting time not yet confirmed.

[d3] Mara Wells has received the full account of the vent system in person — the crystalline residue, the pale seedling, and Aevi's naming of the control prompt. She has warned that the elders will want a
[d4] The dock child has provisionally agreed to tap the six-count rhythm for the elders Thursday — a live sensory witness to the subsurface pulse, independent of Usnea's findings.

evidence-for-the-elders.label: Evidence for the Elders
facts: [d1] Elder meeting confirmed: Thursday at the common house, at the last of afternoon light — two days from now. Mara Wells is coordinating. All three evidence items must be ready: pale seedling (held), fil

facts: [d1] The contamination enters the eastern irrigation arm through a slow warm seep beneath a Precursor-adjacent retaining wall at the arm's bend around a granite outcropping. The seep carries warmth with no

precursor-geometry-in-silt.label: Geometric Patterning Beneath the Seep

seep-geometry-six-fold.label: The Six-Fold Pattern

[d2] The silt record of the six-fold radial geometry was destroyed when the bank collapsed during a marking attempt. The warm seep itself continues unchanged. The site is now unmarked — Usnea is its only r

the-six-fold-pattern.label: The Six-Fold Pattern

[d3] Prism sight reading of the vent aperture confirms the six-fold geometry is present at all three levels — seep, basin, and now the vent source. The heat at the vent is calibrated: six arms, equal tempe

[d3] Aevi holds an internal record of the six-fold geometry — function identified as 'distribution': equal-tempered, pressurized, designed to spread its contents everywhere equally, not concentrate them. T

[d3] The six interface depressions at the vent aperture rim are hexagonal in cross-section, tapering to a point — seats for keys or pins, removed deliberately by compression rather than lost to time. The s

[d3] The interface depressions at the vent aperture contain crystalline nanite-structured residue — light-holding rather than light-reflecting, pre-Transition manufacture per Aevi's reading. The material w

[d3] Full perimeter survey of the vent rim's six interface depressions: all six were used and deliberately cleared. Depressions 3 and 6 — diametrically opposite each other — both hold trace crystalline res

[d3] Aevi identifies the six-seat ring as a control architecture with a specific logic: two diametrically opposite seats (third and sixth) set the system's operational mode; the remaining four are paramete

[d3] The crystalline residue from the third depression answered the sixth seat when held close — confirming it is sixth-seat material, the origin key (placed last, removed first in operation). The third se

[d3] The vent's periodic pause is not a fault cycle — it is an interface prompt. The system is waiting for the third-and-sixth key sequence to resolve. It has been waiting, patiently, for a very long time.

[d4] The Lower Terrace junction-stone at the Heights gateline is a node in the same system as the seep and vent aperture — residual warmth of identical quality, pulsing in the vent's control-prompt rhythm.

[d4] The Lower Terrace gateline warden saw the channel light once before — three springs back, before holding his post. A Heights-side witness to the system's activity now exists; his full account is unsha

seep-delivery-path.label: The Seep's Delivery Path

facts: [d2] A deliberate feeder channel at the wall's fifth course — three finger-widths wide, upper lip dressed smooth in the same style as the valve stone — connects the upper-terrace side of the wall to direct

seep-mechanism-continuous.label: The Mechanism Does Not Sleep
facts: [d2] The feeder channel's hiss was audible throughout the night — the delivery mechanism runs continuously, independent of daylight or human activity. Whatever drives the flow above the wall is not passive

feeder-trough-upper-terrace.label: The Feeder Trough — Upper Source
facts: [d2] The feeder channel's upper mouth is a dressed stone trough set flush with the fallow upper terrace floor, angling down through the wall at a deliberate decline. Gray-green water moves through it slowl

contamination-basin-source.label: The Source Basin
facts: [d2] The feeder trough's upslope end terminates at a dry-fitted circular stone basin (~2 paces across) flush with the upper terrace floor. Gray-green water upwells from a central aperture under pressure —

basin-source-unknown.label: Basin Source: Above the Scree

millbrook-common-house.label: Millbrook Common House

the-counting-river.label: The Counting River

harmonic-heights-lower-terrace.label: Harmonic Heights — Lower Terrace
facts: [d4] The Lower Terrace gateline is the entry point for valley-floor visitors. Radiant-origin travelers are met with practiced neutrality at best — wardens have been told to allow entry but haven't arrived

[d4] The Lower Terrace gateline has a stone-cut irrigation channel that, on Day 4 afternoon, showed a ribbon of pre-Transition light traveling upstream and sinking through a precisely straight crack in a j

sela-and-pell.label: Sela and Pell

establishedFacts.text: Usnea Beard has arrived at the Harmonic Heights Lower Terrace — first visit, carrying contamination evidence. Deep suspicion of Radiant visitors is the ambient social pressure at the gate.

The junction-stone crack at the Lower Terrace channel trough carries residual warmth matching the vent-aperture's six-fold heat signature, and pulses in the vent control-prompt rhythm — it is a node i

The Lower Terrace gateline warden witnessed the channel light three springs ago, before he held his current post — a Heights-side witness to the pre-Transition system's activity predating Usnea's inve
The dock child has confirmed the subsurface rhythmic pulse counts to six before a long pause — matching the vent aperture's six-seat architecture — and has grown stronger in character since the water
The dock child has provisionally agreed to tap the six-count rhythm for Mara Wells and the Millbrook elders at Thursday's meeting — a live witness, pending confirmation.
The dock child's name is Sela. Her mother is Pell, who sells cord and hemp at Millbrook's east market stall. Pell's permission is needed for Sela to testify at Thursday's elder meeting.
Mara Wells has committed to approaching Pell at the east market stall at dawn on Day 5 to secure permission for Sela's testimony at Thursday's elder meeting.
